policy_statement: Convene a work group to establish a well-resourced campaign to build student/public awareness of climate change effects/solutions, provide workforce/early career opportunities, and support beneficial lifestyle changes.
mechanism_description: The State convenes a work group to develop a campaign that builds climate change awareness and workforce opportunities, aiming to encourage beneficial lifestyle changes that reduce emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Information/norms campaign → behavior change in energy/transport/consumption → ↓ fuel/energy demand & ↓ high-emissions activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public/students/workforce are targeted with coordinated climate education, awareness messaging, and engagement opportunities intended to shift daily choices (travel mode, home energy use, purchasing, diet, waste practices) and potentially increase participation in climate-friendly programs.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Behavioral shifts reduce combustion of fossil fuels and other emissions sources across sectors (electricity and heat demand, vehicle miles traveled, material consumption/waste), lowering CO₂ and other GHG emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in economy-wide GHG emissions (potentially also enabling longer-run decarbonization via increased public support, though the core mechanism is direct behavior-driven emissions reduction).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions due to voluntary behavior changes prompted by climate awareness and engagement.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Awareness/engagement → behavior change → reduced emitting activities/energy use → ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some behaviors may improve “efficiency,” the mechanism is not primarily technical efficiency improvements; it is demand-side emissions reduction through behavior and social change. It is also not adaptation because it does not primarily reduce exposure/vulnerability to climate hazards.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a campaign is framed as “resilience preparedness” (heat safety, flood readiness), it would shift toward Adaptation; if it is framed mainly as “waste prevention” or “efficiency tips,” it could overlap with Resource Efficiency—but the dominant climate effect across instances of this canonical mechanism remains emissions mitigation via behavior change.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is public climate awareness leading to lifestyle/behavior changes intended to cut emissions fits Mitigation as the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., transport demand reduction vs household energy conservation vs consumption/waste) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to the policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Campaigns that primarily promote hazard preparedness/resilience behaviors (rather than emissions-cutting behaviors) would be Adaptation; campaigns narrowly focused on specific efficiency actions (e.g., home retrofits) may be better treated under Resource Efficiency if the dominant pathway becomes “same service → less energy input,” but for this canonical mechanism the dominant intent/effect is broad emissions reduction via behavior change.
key_indicators: “convene a work group”; “establish a … campaign”; “build student and public awareness of climate change effects and solutions”; “effect beneficial lifestyle changes”; “workforce development and early career development opportunities”
co_benefits: Workforce development/job pipeline; general environmental literacy/civic engagement; potential public health benefits from lower-emission lifestyle shifts (e.g., active transport, improved air quality)
instance_edge_case_notes: “Information-disclosure” is used here to cover education/awareness/social marketing campaigns; the text does not specify any mandatory disclosure requirements. The “convene a work group” element also resembles a planning/strategy step, but the dominant instrument is the awareness campaign.
